# Questions in Livestream 195

Live Stream #195: Wisdom of the Channel

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=558" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:18</a> | What did Egyptian beauty standards value? | He said the evidence points more to economic and social signals such as being well fed than to a race-based ideal. | He cited a text from Hatshepsut's bark shrine that praises large women of Kush and explained that size signaled wealth, food security, and not living at the edge of subsistence. He also distinguished male ideals from female ideals, saying Egyptian men were expected to look athletic while larger women were praised as beautiful. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=749" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:29</a> | What do you wish people understood better about patristics? | He stressed the diversity of views among the church fathers and warned against treating one father's opinion as universal. | He said the most important thing is to read widely because the fathers disagreed on many issues and cannot be reduced to a single proof-texted voice. He also emphasized the scale of the corpus, noting that much remains untranslated or hard to access, so no one should pretend that one quoted father represents every early Christian writer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=1162" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:22</a> | What is going on with 1 Samuel 13:1 on Bible Hub and with 1 Samuel 13 generally? | He called the verse highly corrupt and said every translation has to emend it; Bible Hub appears to follow one Septuagint-related reading. | He described 1 Samuel 13:1 as one of the most corrupted biblical texts because the preserved wording does not make coherent sense. Since the damage may come from scribal copying or a damaged scroll, translators must reconstruct something, and he treated the Septuagint-related solution as one early attempt rather than a definitive original reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=1552" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:52</a> | What lost biblical texts do you most wish we still had? | He especially mentioned the real Book of Jasher and the Book of the Wars of the Lord. | He said the real Book of Jasher, not later forgeries under that name, would be valuable because it would show what biblical authors were quoting. He also mentioned records such as the Book of the Wars of the Lord and royal annals behind Kings, clarifying that such sources would be fascinating for comparison but would not become Scripture merely because Scripture cites them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=1725" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:45</a> | What is the difference between being lucky to get a destruction layer and destruction layers being uncommon? | He distinguished preservation from excavation luck. Destruction layers are useful corroborating evidence when found, but their absence does not automatically disprove an event. | He said finding a destruction layer is a gift because it gives corroborating evidence for a destruction, but not every conquest produces a burned layer and not every layer survives later site leveling. Avaris was his example of a conquered city without a clear burn layer, so absence of that evidence can reflect how the conquest happened or what later archaeology preserved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=2040" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:00</a> | Does he remember the stream where he and IP responded to Bowen on the number of Israelites? | He did not remember the specific stream and suggested searching IP's channel for the discussion. | He said he has appeared on many other channels and does not keep a detailed memory of what he said in each interview. Since there were only a few appearances on IP's channel, he advised checking that channel directly to identify the relevant response. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=2091" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:51</a> | What are his thoughts on The Book of Moses Revisited, Egypt and the Old Testament, and A Christian's Guide to Evidence for the Bible? | He had not read those exact books, knew and liked Paul Lawrence's other work, did not know Jeff Morrow, and had read and liked a different J. Daniel Hays book. | He was careful not to evaluate books he had not read. He said he knows Paul Lawrence and likes his work, had no basis to comment on Jeff Morrow, and had not read Hays's evidence book, though he liked Hays's book on the temple and tabernacle enough to consider adding it to the channel bibliography. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=2247" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:27</a> | Why might a bookstore stop selling Hays's books on Joshua and Judges? | He said it could be related to Hays taking positions that conflict with some early-date expectations, but presented that as speculation. | He speculated that Hays may lean toward a late Exodus date or other positions that make some conservative institutions uneasy, but he stressed that Hays had not necessarily said this explicitly. He broadened the point to institutional pressure, saying scholars often avoid direct statements on contested positions because angry donors or readers can create professional trouble. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=2481" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:21</a> | How can someone with several unpopular theological and historical positions make even more people hate them? | The response was mostly joking, but the serious point was that truth can draw hostility and one should not seek hatred for its own sake. | He joked about criticizing figures and views that provoke strong reactions, including Ron Wyatt, King James Only arguments, flat earth claims, the Shroud of Turin, and Marian apparitions. The serious answer was that speaking truth can bring hostility even when done in love, but he warned against seeking hatred because being hated is not enjoyable or desirable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=2681" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:41</a> | What is blasphemy against the Holy Spirit? | He described it as a final rejection of the Spirit's witness to Christ rather than an ordinary stumble in speech. | He defined the Spirit's role as exposing the truth about Christ and glorifying him, then described blasphemy against the Spirit as knowing and rejecting that truth. He connected it with apostasy and Hebrews 6, where someone has been enlightened, tasted the gift, and then falls away in a way that cannot be renewed to repentance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=2766" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:06</a> | How does one atone for blasphemy against the Holy Spirit? | He said one cannot atone for it because he treats the sin as final apostasy or decisive rejection of Christ. | He said there is no separate atonement for this because the person has accepted and then decisively rejected Christ, so Christ is not crucified again for that person. He read Hebrews 6 as describing someone who received real benefits of the Spirit and the word of God but then fell away, making repentance itself impossible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=3098" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:38</a> | Is blasphemy against the Holy Spirit similar to what demons did? | He said it is similar in that demons knew the truth and rejected it, though angelic beings are in a different category from humans. | He agreed that the comparison works at the level of knowing the truth and rejecting it, but he qualified that angelic beings start from a different state than humans. Demons did not need repentance from human sin in the same way, but they knowingly rejected the truth and held it up to ridicule, which makes the analogy meaningful. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=3144" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:24</a> | Is it knowing the truth about Jesus but choosing to mock it? | He agreed, emphasizing intentional, malicious mockery rather than a lighthearted or ignorant mistake. | He said the issue is not an ignorant or lighthearted mistake but open, intentional, malicious mockery after someone has known and participated in the benefits of the truth. He warned that ministry to people who have fully deconverted and mock the faith may be unusually difficult, while people acting on limited knowledge are in a different category. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=3529" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:49</a> | Does this apply to Paul before his Damascus road conversion? | He treated Paul as a special case: Paul opposed Christians but was zealous for God and responded when confronted by Christ. | He said Paul persecuted believers and did not yet believe the Christian message, but he was not simply resistant to known truth in the same way. Paul was zealous for God, though misguided, and when Christ confronted him on the Damascus road he saw his error instead of continuing in hardened rejection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=3634" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:34</a> | What if someone feels their salvation cannot be authentic because they left the faith and came back? | He distinguished drifting from deliberate apostasy and said returning to faith is evidence that the person had not finally rejected Christ. | He said coming back is itself evidence that the person had not finally apostatized, and he called that faith tested rather than inauthentic. He distinguished a person who drifts away and later returns from someone who knowingly leaves and mocks the faith, using Hebrews 2's drifting image rather than Hebrews 6 for the former case. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=4023" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:03</a> | Does 1 Samuel 4:5-10 preserve evidence that the ten plagues tradition was already known? | He treated the Philistines' fear there as good support for an early Exodus memory rather than a convenient late invention. | He read the passage and noted that the Philistines know a version of Israel's plague story, even if they locate the plagues imprecisely in the wilderness rather than Egypt. Against a source-critical reading that would make this a late insertion, he argued it makes sense as secondhand reputation spreading through earlier Israelite-Philistine conflict. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=4528" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:28</a> | When did Ishtar worship fall out of favor? | He placed the decline with Christianity's spread into Mesopotamia, beginning around the second century AD, with remnants lingering later. | He said Ishtar worship survived in Mesopotamia into the Christian era but began declining as Christianity penetrated the region around the second century AD. He placed its final gasps around the fifth century AD, after the older Persian-era religious structures had weakened. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=4577" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:17</a> | In John 7:8, did Jesus lie when he later went to the feast? | He said no, reading Jesus as rejecting his brothers' timing and public program rather than making an absolute statement that he would never go. | He framed the passage by noting that Jesus was avoiding Judea because people wanted to kill him and that his brothers were urging a public display despite not believing in him. He read Jesus' statement as refusing to go according to their timing and agenda, not as a false promise never to attend the feast at all. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=5033" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:53</a> | What is a reasonable daily budget for food, clothes, and souvenirs in Egypt? | He suggested roughly $50-100 US per day for incidentals, cautioned that prices had risen, and recommended scarves and belts over most clothing. | He warned that prices had changed with inflation and used current food prices as a rough gauge before recommending about $50 to $100 per day in US dollars for incidental spending. He said tours often cover some meals, discouraged buying ordinary clothes, and suggested locally made belts, scarves, and similar items as better purchases. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=6268" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:44:28</a> | Did everyone write today? | He said he had done line editing and encouraged creative writing as a way to engage culture. | He said he had been line editing his second book and treated writing or editing as part of cultural engagement. He argued that theology and biblical knowledge are like the unseen part of an iceberg, while stories, art, drama, video, and other creative work are the visible forms through which people often encounter Christian ideas. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=7483" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:43</a> | What was his very first job? | He said his first regular job was babysitting for a family whose youngest child had health problems; later he worked in a produce packing plant. | He clarified that his first regular paid work was babysitting at about age seventeen, partly because the family needed someone with first-aid certification for a child with health problems. His later company job was at Western Greenhouse Growers, where he packed tomatoes, cucumbers, and peppers and learned about many varieties of bell peppers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=8022" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:42</a> | Can he steelman the idea that King Josiah authored the Old Testament? | He rejected the theory as implausible, arguing that major temples had texts and that the claim depends on prior critical assumptions. | He refused to steelman it because he saw it as a theory driven by prior commitment to the documentary hypothesis rather than evidence. He said Josiah finding a Torah scroll in the temple is not suspicious by itself because major temples had texts and libraries, so the Temple of the Lord would naturally have textual materials. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=8261" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:41</a> | Are there parallels between modern cultural Christianity and pre-exilic Judaism in failing to live out the faith? | He saw a closer parallel with early Roman Christianity, where many people were culturally connected to the church without deeply understanding the faith. | He said the pre-exilic Judah comparison is too strong unless people are openly mixing Christianity with idol worship, though some liberal churches may approach that. His preferred analogy was early Roman Christianity, where a minority were well formed while many others were culturally attached to the church without much understanding of the faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=8447" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:47</a> | Is any sense of inerrancy necessary? | He said it is not necessary for salvation, but it is important for sound exegesis because it keeps the interpreter from sitting in judgment over the text. | He described inerrancy as a foundational dogma tied to the idea that copies point back to an inerrant original, while admitting that a person can be Christian without affirming it. For exegesis, however, he argued that rejecting inerrancy tends to make interpreters more willing to declare the text wrong instead of working carefully with authorial intent. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=9124" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:04</a> | Why are the verbs in Genesis 2:5-6 imperfect and not perfect? | He connected the imperfect forms to incomplete aspect and said English translations often render that sense with past-tense wording such as "not yet." | He explained the issue through aspect rather than simple tense, saying the imperfect reflects incomplete or ongoing action. In Genesis 2:5-6, he paraphrased the sense as shrubs not yet being in the earth, plants not yet sprouting, rain not yet falling, and no man yet cultivating the ground, while noting that wooden English would sound awkward. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=9501" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:38:21</a> | How should someone handle perceived Christian theological contradictions that are pushing them toward theism? | He argued that many alleged contradictions are better treated as resolvable inconsistencies and urged looking for long-standing answers. | He said bare theism will not solve specifically Christian theological worries and that many alleged contradictions are perceived inconsistencies with long-standing answers. Once a possible resolution exists, he said the issue is no longer a strict contradiction, and he gave examples where incomplete or selective wording can be mistaken for direct conflict. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=9917" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:17</a> | If Jesus could be around sinners without destroying them, why is Mary considered sinless by the argument that God cannot be in the presence of sin? | He rejected that argument, saying kenosis means Christ did not exercise divine judgment against every sinner he encountered. | He argued that Christ's self-emptying includes forgoing the exercise of divine judgment and royal prerogative during his earthly ministry. If mere contact with sinners required immediate judgment, then everyone Jesus touched, including the woman who touched his garment, would have been destroyed, so he saw the argument for Mary's sinlessness as a poor use of kenosis and the text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=10160" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:20</a> | Is the Egyptian context for Isaiah 9:6 a pharaonic fivefold titulary? | He was doubtful, noting that he counted four titles and that the expected Egyptian royal-name prefixes are absent. | He counted the Isaiah titles as four, unless "wonderful counselor" is split in a way he did not accept, and therefore doubted the fivefold titulary claim. He also noted that Egyptian royal titulary normally includes recognizable prefixes such as Horus, Two Ladies, Golden Horus, prenomen, and nomen markers, which he did not see in Isaiah 9. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=10420" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:40</a> | Does Mary Byrne's argument that a fifth Isaiah 9 title was lost solve the four-title problem? | He called that special pleading unless there is evidence the fifth title was ever present. | He said the proposal that a fifth title was lost is ad hoc unless there is evidence that such a title ever existed in the text. He was open to Egyptian influence where it is actually present, but argued that using a lost title to rescue the theory risks reading the parallel into the passage rather than deriving it from the evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=11436" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:10:36</a> | What are good sources for Egyptian and ancient Near Eastern reliefs? | He recommended University of Chicago/Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures and Chicago House publications, many of which make temple reliefs available. | He pointed to the University of Chicago's former Oriental Institute, now the Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures, and especially Chicago House publications. He said many of these publications are freely available online and include reliefs from major Egyptian sites such as Karnak, Medinet Habu, and Luxor Temple. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=11744" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:15:44</a> | How should Christians deal with the violent and troubling past of people who claimed to be Christian? | He said to ask whether the acts accord with Christianity's teachings; atrocities done in defiance of Christ's commands should not be treated as the faith's ideal. | He said Christians should distinguish between what Christianity teaches and what people claiming the name have actually done. Atrocities committed in defiance of commands to love one's neighbor and enemy show disobedience to the faith, not the faith's highest ideal, and he illustrated this with people who publicly claim Christian identity while living contrary to it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=12186" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:06</a> | How does Silence frame martyrdom versus hiding under persecution? | He said Endo's book is better than the film and deliberately leaves the tension unresolved, showing both martyrdom and hidden compromise as difficult options. | He praised Shusaku Endo's novel and said it asks whether it is better to accept martyrdom under overwhelming persecution or to hide. He emphasized that Endo does not make either option easy or ideal: martyrdom can end the witnesser's ability to teach, while hiddenness or outward apostasy is also morally troubling. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=13107" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:38:27</a> | Was the Tower of Babel a ziggurat? | He said yes, most likely, though probably not an especially tall one. | He answered that Babel was most likely a ziggurat from a period associated with ziggurat building. He added that it probably was not especially tall and that the text is often misread as if the builders expected to reach heaven physically, when the language is better understood as reaching up toward the heavens. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=14343" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:59:03</a> | How similar or different were ancient and medieval siege tactics and technology? | He said both used things like walls, arrows, slings, battering rams, and siege towers, while later medieval tools such as trebuchets, crossbows, and ballistas were not yet available in the conquest period. | He said ancient armies had battering rams, arrows, slings, wall-scaling, and siege towers, with Egyptians developing siege towers by the Middle Kingdom. The main difference he named was later medieval technology, since trebuchets, crossbows, and ballistas were not available in the Israelite conquest period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=14461" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:01:01</a> | Did he read Sentinel Apologetics' funny Christmas post on YouTube? | He said he had not read it yet because he had been busy, but would look for it. | He answered briefly that he had not seen it because he had been busy and had not been reading many extra posts. He thanked the commenter for the support and said he would look for the post. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=14499" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:01:39</a> | Would Genesis 2:16 be better translated "eat you will eat" from every tree of the garden? | He said "you will eat" and "may eat" can both be acceptable because the Hebrew imperfect has aspectual scope and can carry a permissive sense. | He checked the Hebrew and identified the verb as a qal imperfect second masculine singular, so "you will eat" is a possible rendering. Because imperfect aspect can carry a permissive sense in context, he said "may eat" is also acceptable even if "you will eat" may be more formally precise. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=14636" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:03:56</a> | Was Phinehas the Nubian actually Nubian? | He said the Egyptian-derived name means "the Nubian," so Nubian ancestry is possible, though he would not say Phinehas was necessarily fully Nubian. | He traced Phinehas to an Egyptian name meaning "the Nubian" and said that makes some Nubian ancestry possible. He noted that Nubians were present at Avaris, including a Nubian infantry division and Nubian burials, so a mixed Israelite and Nubian background is plausible, though he would not claim Phinehas was fully Nubian. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=15644" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:20:44</a> | In Jeremiah 17:10, is "I the Lord search the heart and test the kidneys" analogical language? | He said yes: in Hebrew thought the heart is associated with thinking and the kidneys with emotions, comparable to a modern "gut feeling." | He explained that in Hebrew thought the heart is associated with thought and the kidneys with emotions, so the wording is analogical rather than anatomical precision. He compared the idiom to modern language about a "gut feeling," where bodily language describes inward perception or emotion without claiming the organ literally thinks. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=15878" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:24:38</a> | Where did the Essenes derive their two-messiah idea, one of Moses and one of Aaron, and could it come from Isaiah? | He doubted Isaiah was the source and suggested it was more likely an eisegetical reading into Isaiah. | He said the Essenes did not always hold views orthodox by wider Jewish standards and that multiple-messiah expectations were not entirely alien in first-century Judaism. He doubted Isaiah itself taught the idea and suggested they more likely read the Moses-and-Aaron scheme into Isaiah rather than deriving it cleanly from the text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=16090" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:28:10</a> | When did his hair turn gray? | He answered jokingly that it turned gray when he got old, essentially when he moved back to Vancouver. | He gave a joking answer rather than a precise age, saying it happened when he got old. He then connected it loosely with the stressful period when he moved back to Vancouver after looking for other work and being forced to return to Canada. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=16844" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:40:44</a> | Does Romans say God works all things for your good? | He corrected the phrasing, saying the text is about all things working together for good, not necessarily for one's personal good. | He said the phrase should not be treated as a promise that every event benefits the individual believer personally. His distinction was that all things work together for "good" in a qualitative or kingdom sense, so even martyrdom may serve the good of Christ's kingdom without being comfortable or beneficial to the person in a narrow sense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vd2HbEVjess?t=17611" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:53:31</a> | Is it a good idea to reference real-world mythology or events in a book set in an alternate Earth with its own mythology and history? | He said it can be done, including alternate histories and mythological references, as long as the work's overall worldview remains properly ordered. | He said alternate histories and real-world mythological references can work in fiction, including references to pagan gods or other myth systems. The governing concern is that the story's worldview remain ordered under a Christian frame, even subtly, so such myths are not treated as ultimate over Yahweh or Christ. |
